name: monthly-trend
whatItMeasures: The 10-Month SMA Rule is a simple timing strategy that compares an asset's monthly closing price to its 10-month simple moving average. When the monthly close is above the 10-month SMA, the asset is in an uptrend and you remain invested. When the monthly close is below the 10-month SMA, the asset is in a downtrend and you move to cash or bonds. This is a long-term trend filter designed for monthly rebalancing.

parameters.name: Period
parameters.rationale: The 10-month SMA (approximately equivalent to the 200-day SMA) has been extensively researched by Meb Faber and others. It captures the long-term trend while filtering out short-term noise. Monthly evaluation reduces transaction costs and whipsaws.

howToRead: Monthly close above the 10-month SMA: the long-term trend is up — remain invested in equities.
Monthly close below the 10-month SMA: the long-term trend is down — move to cash or bonds for capital preservation.
This is a binary system evaluated once per month at the close — no intra-month decisions needed.
The rule's value is primarily in avoiding the worst drawdowns, not in generating excess returns.
During whipsaw periods (price oscillating around the SMA), expect occasional false signals — this is the cost of downside protection.

signalRules.buy: Monthly closing price is above the 10-month SMA — stay invested or re-enter the market.
signalRules.sell: Monthly closing price is below the 10-month SMA — exit to cash or bonds until the trend reverses.
signalRules.neutral: Price is very close to the 10-month SMA — the trend is ambiguous; maintain current positioning and re-evaluate next month.

limitations: The rule generates whipsaw signals during extended sideways markets, leading to small repeated losses.
Monthly evaluation means you can lose up to one month of downside before the signal triggers.
In strong V-shaped recoveries, the rule can keep you out of the market during the initial bounce.
Tax implications of moving between equities and cash can reduce after-tax returns.
The rule is designed for broad market indices — it may be less effective for individual stocks with higher idiosyncratic volatility.
evidence: Meb Faber's research, published in 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation' (2006, updated periodically), demonstrates that the 10-month SMA rule applied to the S&P 500 reduces maximum drawdown from approximately 55% (buy-and-hold) to approximately 13%, while delivering comparable long-term returns. The strategy was invested in equities roughly 70% of the time, avoiding the worst bear markets. This dramatic drawdown reduction — from 55% to 13% — is the rule's primary value: making equity investing psychologically and financially survivable for long-term investors.
